ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Bak Kut Teh yee mee. FOODON_03302051 (noodle soup food product) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKM (Noodle soup) used as closest parent; no specific Bak Kut Teh yee mee entry exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Bak Kut Teh yee mee or equivalent noodle soup dish.

main_ingredients: Chinese herbs (dang gui, cinnamon, star anise, cloves, pepper)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Black Bak Kut Teh Yee Mee weighing approximately 450 g, comprising a portion of yee mee noodles, two to three pork rib pieces, and a generous serving of dark herbal broth.

nutrient_source.title: Bak Kut Teh (pork rib soup) and Yee Mee nutrient data — adapted composite estimate from HPB Singapore Foodo and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single HPB Foodo entry exists for Black Bak Kut Teh Yee Mee as a combined dish. Nutrient values are derived by compositing: (1) HPB Foodo entry for Bak Kut Teh (pork rib soup, Teochew/dark style) providing broth and pork rib macros, and (2) USDA SR Legacy entry 20113 (egg noodles, cooked) for the yee mee noodle component. Proportions estimated at approximately 40% noodles, 35% broth, 25% pork ribs by weight. Values represent a plausible per-100g composite for the assembled dish. Confidence is medium due to the absence of a direct laboratory analysis of this specific restaurant preparation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of egg noodles and mixed noodle soup dishes — analogous estimate based on Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC (2008) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Black Bak Kut Teh Yee Mee. GI estimated at 40 based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for egg noodles (GI approximately 35–46, mean ~40) and the diluting effect of the protein-rich pork rib broth and fat content, which lower the overall glycaemic response of the dish. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (40 × 60.75) / 100 = 24.3, where carbohydrate per 450 g serving = 13.5 × 4.5 = 60.75 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from dark soy sauce and pork broth
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit consumption of Black Bak Kut Teh Yee Mee due to its estimated sodium content of approximately 480 mg per 100 g (approximately 2160 mg per bowl), which can substantially contribute to the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg; requesting reduced soy sauce and consuming less broth are practical mitigation strategies.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from yee mee noodles partially offset by protein and fat
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should be aware that a standard bowl of Black Bak Kut Teh Yee Mee carries an estimated glycemic load of approximately 24, which is in the moderate-to-high range; portion control of the noodle component and pairing with the protein-rich pork ribs can help moderate postprandial blood glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from pork ribs and elevated sodium from dark soy broth
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume Black Bak Kut Teh Yee Mee in moderation given its saturated fat content from pork ribs (approximately 1.4 g per 100 g) and high sodium load from the dark soy-based broth, both of which are risk factors for adverse cardiovascular outcomes; choosing leaner cuts and reducing broth intake is advised.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Black Bak Kut Teh Yee Mee is a Singaporean noodle soup dish that combines the rich, dark, herbal pork rib soup known as Bak Kut Teh with yee mee (yi mian), a type of pre-fried egg noodle that absorbs broth readily. The Teochew-style Bak Kut Teh broth is characterised by a lighter, peppery profile, whereas the Hokkien or Malaysian-influenced black variant uses dark soy sauce, garlic, and a blend of Chinese medicinal herbs including dang gui (angelica root), cinnamon, star anise, and cloves, producing a deeply coloured, savoury, and slightly sweet broth. The pork ribs are slow-simmered until tender, yielding a gelatinous, umami-rich soup. Yee mee noodles, made from wheat flour and egg, have a soft, slightly chewy texture and a mild flavour that complements the bold broth. The dish is typically served as a complete one-bowl meal with optional garnishes such as fried shallots, chopped spring onions, and a side of dark soy sauce with chilli. Nutritionally, the dish provides moderate protein from pork ribs, carbohydrates from the noodles, and a notable sodium load from the dark soy sauce and broth. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 40 (low), moderated by the protein and fat content. Clinically, the high sodium content is the primary concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease, while the moderate glycemic load warrants portion awareness for those with Type 2 Diabetes. This dish is not suitable for halal diets due to the pork content and is not vegetarian or vegan.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Black Bak Kut Teh Yee Mee as a combined dish. Nutrient values are a composite estimate derived from HPB Singapore Foodo (Bak Kut Teh broth and pork rib component) and USDA SR Legacy (egg noodles, cooked). GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for egg noodles. Medium confidence reflects the absence of direct laboratory analysis for this specific restaurant preparation.
